Becket's murder made Canterbury England's foremost medieval pilgrimage destination. UNESCO inscribed the site in 1988. The cathedral survived World War II Baedeker Blitz bombing relatively intact, though surrounding streets were devastated in raids specifically targeting England's historic cathedral cities as acts of cultural warfare.

name: Pompeii and Herculaneum
description: Two Roman cities buried by Mount Vesuvius in 79 AD and preserved intact under volcanic ash for nearly 1,700 years. Pompeii's streets, homes, bakeries, theatres, fast-food counters and brothels offer the most complete picture of everyday Roman life anywhere in existence. Plaster casts of victims frozen in their final moments and 2,000-year-old graffiti create an intimacy with the ancient world unmatched at any other archaeological site on Earth, drawing over 3.5 million visitors annually.
history: The eruption of Vesuvius on 24 August 79 AD buried both cities within hours. Pompeii was rediscovered in 1748 and systematic excavation began under Charles III of Spain. These excavations established the practice of stratigraphic archaeological excavation — Pompeii essentially created modern archaeology as a scientific discipline. UNESCO inscribed the Archaeological Areas of Pompeii, Herculaneum and Torre Annunziata as a World Heritage Site in 1997.

name: Great Barrier Reef
description: The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, stretching 2,300 km along Australia's Queensland coast — larger than the United Kingdom and visible from space. The world's biggest structure made by living organisms, it shelters over 1,500 fish species, 4,000 mollusc species and six sea turtle species. A UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1981, it now appears on UNESCO's 'In Danger' list due to repeated mass bleaching events caused by rising ocean temperatures from climate change.
history: Inhabited by Aboriginal Australians and Torres Strait Islanders for at least 60,000 years. James Cook ran aground on the reef in 1770. UNESCO inscribed it in 1981. Mass bleaching events in 1998, 2002, 2016, 2017, 2020 and 2022 have each killed large portions of the reef, placing its long-term survival under serious scientific concern and making it a central and urgent symbol of the global climate change crisis and the consequences of inaction.

name: Kathmandu Valley
description: Kathmandu Valley contains seven UNESCO World Heritage monument zones including Pashupatinath Temple — one of Hinduism's holiest sites — the Boudhanath stupa (one of the world's largest Buddhist stupas), and the medieval royal palace complexes of Kathmandu, Patan and Bhaktapur Durbar Squares. A crossroads of Buddhist and Hindu civilisations for over 2,000 years, the valley produces distinctive Newari art and architecture of extraordinary refinement alongside living heritage traditions that continue entirely uninterrupted today.
history: Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979. The 2015 Gorkha earthquake caused widespread destruction to historic monuments including collapsed towers and severely damaged Durbar Square temples. International restoration efforts mobilised rapidly, but reconstruction has been complex, balancing seismic safety with authenticity. Many monuments remain under restoration a decade later, supported by international conservation agencies and bilateral donor governments providing technical and financial assistance.

name: Hiroshima Peace Memorial
description: The Hiroshima Peace Memorial (Genbaku Dome) is the skeletal ruin of a building left standing near the hypocentre of the world's first atomic bomb detonation on 6 August 1945. The only structure to survive near the blast, it was preserved as a permanent reminder of nuclear devastation and a call for universal disarmament. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1996. Over a million visitors annually come to reflect at this most solemn of world monuments in the heart of a rebuilt and thriving city.
history: The atomic bomb detonated 600 metres above the city at 8:15 am on 6 August 1945, killing an estimated 80,000 people instantly and perhaps 140,000 by the end of the year. City authorities preserved the Genbaku Dome in 1966 despite calls to demolish it. UNESCO's inscription in 1996 was controversially opposed by the United States and China. The surrounding Peace Memorial Park is a major global centre of atomic bomb survivor testimony and anti-nuclear advocacy.

name: Ha Long Bay
description: Ha Long Bay in northeast Vietnam contains nearly 2,000 limestone karst islands rising from the emerald Gulf of Tonkin. Vertical rock faces, caves and hidden lagoons create a seascape of extraordinary beauty spanning 1,553 km². Traditional fishing communities live on floating villages within the bay, maintaining customs and livelihoods continued for generations in this otherworldly environment. It is Vietnam's most recognisable natural attraction and one of Southeast Asia's most photographed and visited landscapes.
history: Ha Long Bay was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1994 for its outstanding scenic beauty. The name means 'descending dragon' in Vietnamese — legend holds that a dragon family descended to protect Vietnam from invaders, their tails creating the islands and channels. The bay saw significant naval action during the Vietnam War. Rapidly expanding cruise tourism has raised serious environmental concerns about waste management and ecological degradation in the enclosed bay system.

name: Kyoto Historic Monuments
description: Kyoto served as Japan's imperial capital for over a thousand years and contains a concentration of UNESCO-listed temples, shrines and gardens unmatched in East Asia. The 17 World Heritage locations include the golden Kinkaku-ji, Ryoan-ji's famous rock garden, the Fushimi Inari shrine with its thousands of vermilion torii gates, and the Nijo Castle of the Tokugawa shoguns. Kyoto was deliberately spared atomic bombing in World War II, preserving this irreplaceable cultural heritage for all humanity.
history: Founded in 794 AD as Heian-kyo, Kyoto was Japan's imperial capital until 1869. The UNESCO inscription in 1994 covers 17 constituent properties representing the full range of Japanese Buddhist and Shinto traditions alongside feudal military architecture. US Secretary of War Henry Stimson successfully argued against including Kyoto on the atomic bomb target list, having visited the city and recognising its extraordinary cultural significance — one of history's most consequential aesthetic and moral judgements.

name: Ngorongoro Conservation Area
description: The Ngorongoro Conservation Area contains the world's largest intact volcanic caldera — 19 km across and 600 m deep — sheltering the densest concentration of wildlife in Africa. Over 25,000 large animals including the Big Five, flamingos and rare black rhinos live on the crater floor year-round, visible from the rim in extraordinary concentration. Adjacent Olduvai Gorge contains some of the world's most important hominin fossil finds, documenting 2 million years of human evolution in the cradle of humanity.
history: Established in 1959 when the Maasai were displaced from the Serengeti for national park creation, with their pastoralism rights preserved in the conservation area. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1979. Mary and Louis Leakey's discoveries at Olduvai Gorge, including the 1959 find of Paranthropus boisei, fundamentally changed the scientific understanding of human origins and made Tanzania a focal point of global paleoanthropology for generations of researchers.

name: Sagrada Família
description: Antoni Gaudí's Sagrada Família in Barcelona is the most visited monument in Spain — a Catholic basilica under continuous construction since 1882 and still unfinished after 140 years. Gaudí devoted the last 43 years of his life to the project. The building's organic forms, hyperboloid structures and symbolic programme integrate Christian theology, Catalan nationalism and natural geometry into an architectural vision of mind-bending originality. Completion is projected for around 2026 — the centenary of Gaudí's death.
history: Construction began in 1882 under architect Francisco de Paula del Villar, who resigned the following year. Gaudí took over in 1883 and transformed the Gothic design entirely. Construction was interrupted by the Spanish Civil War (1936–1939) when anarchists burned Gaudí's workshop destroying his models and drawings. Reconstruction from photographs allowed work to resume. Gaudí was struck by a tram in 1926 and died three days later, having completed only a quarter of the building he considered his life's masterwork.

name: Palace of Versailles
description: The Palace of Versailles and its 800-hectare gardens represent the apotheosis of French absolute monarchy and the most influential palace complex in European history. Louis XIV's creation set the template for royal grandeur imitated across Europe. The Hall of Mirrors — 73 metres of arched windows facing 357 mirrors — became the most famous room in Europe. The formal gardens by André Le Nôtre, with their fountains, canals and sculpture, remain the definitive statement of French formal landscape design and the ideal of order imposed on nature.
history: Construction began in 1661 under Louis XIV who made Versailles the seat of French government. The palace was the royal residence until Louis XVI and Marie Antoinette were forced to return to Paris by a revolutionary mob in October 1789. UNESCO inscribed Versailles as a World Heritage Site in 1979. The German Empire was proclaimed here in 1871, and the Treaty of Versailles ending World War I was signed in the Hall of Mirrors in 1919 — the same room, 48 years apart, bookending a century of European catastrophe.

name: Alhambra, Granada
description: The Alhambra in Granada is the finest surviving example of Moorish architecture in the world — a palace and fortress built by the Nasrid sultans between the 13th and 15th centuries. Its Court of the Lions with slender alabaster columns, geometric tile and plasterwork of the Comares Palace, and Generalife gardens represent an aesthetic experience of extraordinary refinement. The palace's arabesque decoration, muqarnas vaulting and water garden integration represent the apex of Andalusian Islamic art.
history: The Alhambra was the seat of the last Muslim sultanate in Western Europe until the Reconquista expelled the Moors from Granada in January 1492 — the same year Columbus sailed for the Americas, tying together two world-defining events. Washington Irving lived in the Alhambra in 1829 and wrote Tales of the Alhambra, sparking the romantic revival of interest in Moorish Spain across Europe and America. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1984.

name: Ephesus
description: Ephesus on Turkey's Aegean coast was one of the greatest cities of the ancient world — home to 200,000 people at its Roman peak in the 1st and 2nd centuries AD. The Library of Celsus with its ornate two-storey facade, the 25,000-seat theatre, and the Terrace Houses with extraordinary preserved mosaics and frescoes make Ephesus the most complete surviving ancient Greek and Roman city accessible to visitors. The Temple of Artemis here was one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.
history: Founded by Greek colonists around 1000 BC and one of the most important cities of the ancient Mediterranean. St Paul preached here and addressed one of his New Testament letters to the Ephesians. The Virgin Mary is believed by Catholic tradition to have lived her final years near Ephesus. The city was progressively abandoned as its harbour silted up between the 5th and 15th centuries AD. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 2015.

name: Dubrovnik Old City
description: Dubrovnik's Old City is the best-preserved medieval walled city in the Mediterranean — limestone walls, Baroque churches, marble-paved streets and terracotta-roofed palaces enclosed within 2 km of intact medieval fortifications. The Republic of Ragusa, ruling the city from 1358 to 1808, was a remarkable mercantile republic that maintained independence between the Ottoman and Venetian empires through skilled diplomacy. George Bernard Shaw called Dubrovnik 'the pearl of the Adriatic' and that reputation has only grown.
history: Founded in the 7th century and developed as the independent Republic of Ragusa, which abolished slavery in 1416 and had one of Europe's first quarantine systems — remarkable innovations for a medieval city-state. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1979. During the 1991–1992 siege by Yugoslav Army forces, 68% of the Old City buildings were damaged. Restoration over the following decade is considered a model of post-war cultural heritage recovery globally.

name: Rapa Nui National Park
description: Easter Island (Rapa Nui) in the remote South Pacific contains the moai — nearly 1,000 monolithic stone statues averaging 4 metres tall carved by the Rapa Nui people between the 13th and 16th centuries. The statues represent ancestral chiefs and were transported from the Rano Raraku quarry to coastal ceremonial platforms. The island's story of societal collapse from resource depletion has made it a globally cited cautionary tale about ecological overshoot and the fragility of isolated civilisations.
history: Polynesian settlers first arrived around 1200 AD. Population collapse from deforestation, civil war and subsequent European slave raids devastated the Rapa Nui people. Dutch explorer Jacob Roggeveen became the first European to see the island on Easter Sunday 1722. UNESCO inscribed Rapa Nui as a World Heritage Site in 1995. Ongoing negotiations between the Rapa Nui people and the Chilean government concern indigenous sovereignty, repatriation of artefacts from European museums, and site management and revenue-sharing rights.

name: British Museum
description: The British Museum in London holds one of the world's greatest collections of human history and culture, with over 8 million objects spanning two million years. Its permanent collection includes the Rosetta Stone, the Elgin Marbles, Egyptian mummies, the Sutton Hoo helmet and the Lewis Chessmen. Founded in 1753, it was the world's first national public museum. Admission is free and it receives over 6 million visitors annually, making it the UK's most visited cultural attraction and a global icon of encyclopaedic collecting.
history: Founded in 1753 based on the collection of physician Sir Hans Sloane, who bequeathed 71,000 objects to the nation. It opened in 1759, making it the world's first free national public museum. The museum's vast collection reflects Britain's imperial history — calls for repatriation of the Elgin Marbles from Greece, the Benin Bronzes from Nigeria and numerous other objects have intensified significantly in recent decades, creating a defining global debate about colonial-era museum acquisitions and the ethics of cultural heritage.

name: Louvre Museum
description: The Louvre in Paris is the world's most visited museum, welcoming over 9 million visitors annually to its 73,000 m² of galleries. Its nearly 35,000 works on permanent display include the Mona Lisa, Venus de Milo, Winged Victory of Samothrace and the Code of Hammurabi. The museum occupies a former royal palace dating to the 12th century, transformed by I.M. Pei's iconic glass pyramid entrance in 1989 into one of the world's most recognisable and photographed museum buildings.
history: The Louvre Palace was built as a fortress in 1190 by King Philip II and converted to a royal residence in the 16th century. It became a public museum on 10 August 1793 during the French Revolution when the royal collections were opened to citizens for the first time. Napoleon vastly expanded the collection through military conquest across Europe and Egypt, though many works were returned after his defeat at Waterloo. The glass pyramid, initially widely controversial, is now one of Paris's most beloved architectural landmarks.

name: Metropolitan Museum of Art
description: The Metropolitan Museum of Art on New York's Fifth Avenue is one of the world's largest art museums with a permanent collection of over 1.5 million objects spanning 5,000 years. Its encyclopaedic collection covers Egyptian antiquities including a complete Temple of Dendur, European Old Masters, American decorative arts, arms and armour, and contemporary art. The Met's facade along Central Park's Museum Mile stretches four city blocks and anchors one of the world's great cultural destinations drawing millions annually.
history: Founded in 1870 by American citizens who believed the United States deserved a great art museum to rival those of Europe. It opened in 1872 and moved to its current Central Park location in 1880. The museum has been a centre of controversy over acquired antiquities — the Euphronios krater (returned to Italy in 2008) and numerous provenance-disputed objects led to sweeping acquisition policy reforms in the 1990s and 2000s that have influenced the entire international museum community.

name: Egyptian Museum Cairo
description: The Egyptian Museum in Tahrir Square houses the world's most extensive collection of ancient Egyptian antiquities, with over 120,000 items spanning 5,000 years. The treasures of Tutankhamun — including his solid gold death mask — and the Royal Mummy Room housing 27 royal mummies including Ramesses II are the celebrated centrepieces. Built in 1902, it has been supplemented by the Grand Egyptian Museum near Giza, which began a phased opening in 2023 as the world's largest archaeological museum ever constructed.
history: Established in 1835 by Auguste Mariette, French archaeologist appointed by the Egyptian government to combat looting of ancient sites. Tutankhamun's treasures, discovered by Howard Carter in 1922, transformed the museum's international status and made Egyptology a global cultural phenomenon. The Grand Egyptian Museum, under construction since 2002 with capacity for 100,000 artefacts and 5 million annual visitors, represents the most ambitious museum construction project in modern history.

name: Smithsonian Museum of Natural History
description: The Smithsonian National Museum of Natural History on Washington D.C.'s National Mall is one of the world's largest natural history museums with over 145 million specimens. The Hope Diamond — the world's largest blue diamond at 45.52 carats — is its most famous exhibit. Its Ocean Hall, fossil halls and human origins exhibits draw 7 million visitors annually at no charge. The museum's scientific research programme employs hundreds of researchers across biology, geology, anthropology and palaeontology.
history: The Smithsonian Institution was founded in 1846 with a bequest from British scientist James Smithson to create an institution for 'the increase and diffusion of knowledge.' The Natural History Museum opened in its current building in 1910. The Hope Diamond was donated by jeweller Harry Winston in 1958, who famously mailed it to the museum in a plain brown paper package via ordinary post. The museum's collection grows by approximately 500,000 new specimens annually through field research worldwide.

name: Hermitage Museum
description: The State Hermitage Museum in St Petersburg is one of the world's largest and oldest museums, housed in the magnificent Winter Palace and five adjacent buildings along the Neva River. Its collection of over 3 million items includes works by Leonardo da Vinci, Raphael, Rembrandt and the world's finest collection of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist paintings outside France. The Winter Palace's 1,057 rooms and 1,945 windows create a backdrop of imperial splendour unmatched in the museum world.
history: Founded in 1764 by Empress Catherine the Great with the purchase of 225 paintings from a Berlin merchant. The Winter Palace served as the Romanov imperial residence until 1917. During the 872-day Siege of Leningrad in World War II, museum staff lived in the basement protecting the collection while German forces surrounded the city — a story of extraordinary cultural dedication under bombardment that has become one of history's defining narratives of wartime cultural preservation.

name: National Museum of India
description: The National Museum of India in New Delhi houses one of the largest collections of Indian artefacts spanning 5,000 years from the Indus Valley Civilisation to the modern era. Its 200,000 works include Buddhist sculpture, Chola bronzes, Mughal miniature paintings, tribal art, coins and textiles. The Harappan dancing girl — a 4,500-year-old bronze figure from Mohenjo-daro — stands as one of the great sculptures of the ancient world and the museum's most celebrated single object.
history: Inaugurated in 1949 two years after Indian independence. The museum was conceived as a symbol of independent India's connection to its 5,000-year cultural heritage, uniting diverse regional traditions under a common national narrative. Repatriation of Indian artefacts from British and American museums remains an ongoing diplomatic priority for the Indian government, with significant returns of objects achieved in recent years through bilateral negotiation.

name: Museo del Prado
description: The Museo del Prado in Madrid is Spain's national art museum and one of the world's greatest painting collections, displaying over 7,600 paintings. Its unrivalled holdings include Velázquez's Las Meninas (considered by many the world's greatest painting), Goya's disturbing Black Paintings, and Bosch's Garden of Earthly Delights. Flemish and Italian masterpieces from the Habsburg royal collection round out a gathering of Old Masters without equal in any single collection anywhere in the world.
history: The Prado opened as a public museum in 1819, displaying the Spanish royal collection accumulated over three centuries. During the Spanish Civil War, the Republican government arranged for the Prado's masterpieces to be evacuated to Valencia, Barcelona and then Geneva — a remarkable logistical operation that saved the collection from potential destruction in a conflict that devastated much of Spain's cultural infrastructure. Today it receives nearly 4 million visitors annually.

name: Vatican Museums
description: The Vatican Museums contain one of the world's greatest art collections accumulated by the Catholic Church over five centuries. The Sistine Chapel ceiling frescoes painted by Michelangelo between 1508 and 1512 — depicting 343 figures across 520 m² — is the undisputed centrepiece. The Raphael Rooms, Gallery of Maps and Egyptian collections are among 54 galleries receiving over 6 million visitors annually, making Vatican City one of the most visited places on Earth relative to its very small population.
history: The Vatican Museums grew from the collection of Pope Julius II, who placed the ancient sculpture Laocoön and His Sons (discovered in 1506) in a courtyard that became the nucleus of the world's first public sculpture collection. Michelangelo's ceiling took four years to complete, painted lying on scaffolding in a physically demanding feat that matched its artistic ambition. The most recent major restoration of the Sistine Chapel frescoes was completed in 1994 after 14 years of painstaking scientific work.

name: National Palace Museum, Taipei
description: The National Palace Museum in Taipei houses the world's largest collection of Chinese imperial art — nearly 700,000 pieces spanning 8,000 years assembled by the Ming and Qing dynasties and evacuated to Taiwan by the Nationalist government in 1949. Highlights include the jadeite cabbage, the Meat-shaped Stone carved to look exactly like braised pork, and over 150,000 rare books. The Beijing and Taipei museums both claim legitimacy as the true custodian of China's imperial cultural heritage.
history: The collection was established as the Palace Museum in Beijing's Forbidden City in 1925. Following Japan's invasion in 1937, 13,000 crates were moved inland to protect them from bombing. After the Nationalist defeat in the Civil War, Chiang Kai-shek had the most prized 600,000 pieces transported to Taiwan in 1949. The National Palace Museum opened in Taipei in 1965. The split collection remains one of the world's most politically charged and symbolically loaded cultural heritage disputes.

name: Rijksmuseum
description: The Rijksmuseum in Amsterdam houses the finest collection of Dutch Golden Age art in the world. Rembrandt's Night Watch — considered the greatest Dutch painting ever made — occupies its own gallery. Vermeer's The Milkmaid, Delftware ceramics, ship models, dolls' houses and Delft Blue tiles complete a comprehensive portrait of Holland's extraordinary 17th-century cultural flowering. The neo-Gothic and Renaissance Revival building by Pierre Cuypers was extensively renovated and reopened in 2013 to extraordinary international acclaim.
history: Founded in The Hague in 1800 using works from the House of Orange-Nassau and moved to Amsterdam in 1808 at Napoleon's direction. The Night Watch was controversially trimmed to fit its original location, removing figures from all four sides. In 1975 a schizophrenic slashed the painting with a bread knife; in 1990 it was attacked with acid — both times expertly restored. The museum's 10-year renovation (2003–2013) was designed to respect the original building while making it fully accessible to modern visitors.

name: Uffizi Gallery
description: The Uffizi Gallery in Florence houses the finest collection of Italian Renaissance painting anywhere on Earth. Botticelli's The Birth of Venus and Primavera, Leonardo da Vinci's Annunciation, Michelangelo's Doni Tondo and Caravaggio's Medusa are among its masterpieces. The museum occupies the Uffizi Palace built in 1560 by Giorgio Vasari for Cosimo I de' Medici — uffizi means 'offices' — on the banks of the Arno in the heart of Renaissance Florence, the city that shaped Western civilisation.
history: The Uffizi collection was built by the Medici family over two centuries and bequeathed to the people of Florence in 1743 by the last Medici heir, Anna Maria Luisa, on condition it never leave Florence — one of history's first acts of cultural philanthropy directed at the public good rather than dynastic prestige or private collection. In 1993, a Mafia car bomb outside the museum killed five people and damaged several paintings in an attack intended to pressure the Italian government on criminal sentencing.

name: National Museum of China
description: The National Museum of China on Tiananmen Square is the world's most visited museum by annual attendance at approximately 8 million visitors. Its collection of over 1.4 million artefacts spans 5,000 years of Chinese civilisation — from Neolithic jade objects and Shang dynasty bronzes through Tang dynasty pottery horses, Song ceramics and contemporary Chinese art. Its 192,000 m² of floor space makes it one of the largest museum buildings ever constructed anywhere in the world.
history: Formed in 1959 through a merger of the National History Museum and Museum of the Chinese Revolution. Closed for a massive expansion from 2007 to 2011, doubling floor space, designed by the Hamburg-based firm gmp Architekten in collaboration with a Chinese architectural institute. The museum's exhibitions explicitly support the Chinese Communist Party's narrative of national history and the party's role in China's national renewal, making it as much a political institution as a cultural one.

name: National Air and Space Museum
description: The Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum on Washington D.C.'s National Mall is the world's most visited museum with approximately 7 million annual visitors. Its collection includes the Wright Brothers' Flyer, Charles Lindbergh's Spirit of St Louis, the Apollo 11 command module, a touchable moon rock, and spacesuits worn by Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin on the lunar surface. The museum opened in 1976 and chronicles humanity's extraordinary journey from the first controlled flight to deep space exploration.
history: The Smithsonian began collecting aviation artefacts in 1876, acquiring the Wright Brothers' Kitty Hawk Flyer in 1948 after a dispute with British museums over where it would be displayed. The current building opened on 1 July 1976 for America's bicentennial. The Steven F. Udvar-Házy Center near Dulles Airport, opened in 2003, houses the Space Shuttle Discovery, a Concorde, the SR-71 Blackbird and the Enola Gay — the B-29 that dropped the first atomic bomb, one of history's most controversial museum display decisions.

name: NMAAHC Washington D.C.
description: The National Museum of African American History and Culture on Washington D.C.'s National Mall is the world's largest museum dedicated to African American life, history and culture. Opened in 2016 after decades of advocacy, its 37,000 artefacts include Harriet Tubman's hymnal, a slave cabin from South Carolina, Muhammad Ali's boxing robe and Emmett Till's casket. The stunning bronze-latticed building by David Adjaye draws on West African architectural tradition, making it as architecturally significant as it is culturally essential.
history: Legislation to establish a national museum of African American history was first introduced in Congress in 1915 — it took 89 years and six Congressional attempts before President George W. Bush signed the authorising legislation in 2003. President Barack Obama broke ground in 2012 and opened the completed museum in 2016. The museum received over 3 million visitors in its first year — more than any other Smithsonian museum — demonstrating the profound public appetite for this long-overlooked history being finally told at national scale.

name: Acropolis Museum
description: The Acropolis Museum at the foot of the Athenian Acropolis was designed specifically to house the sculptures and artefacts of the Acropolis. The building by Bernard Tschumi, opened in 2009, places the Parthenon Gallery on the top floor aligned precisely with the Parthenon visible through floor-to-ceiling glass — a deliberate architectural argument for reuniting the sculptures. Original Caryatid porch figures, Nike temple frieze blocks and surviving Parthenon sculptures are displayed in their original ceremonial sequence.
history: The Acropolis Museum was designed to demonstrate that Greece has a world-class facility capable of housing the Elgin Marbles — addressing the British Museum's long-standing argument that Athens lacked adequate infrastructure for their return. Construction was delayed repeatedly by archaeological discoveries during excavation; the foundations were redesigned to span over the revealed ancient city beneath the site. The museum's opening reignited global debate about the repatriation of cultural heritage acquired during the colonial era.

name: Iziko South African Museum
description: The Iziko South African Museum in Cape Town is the oldest museum in sub-Saharan Africa, founded in 1825, and houses southern Africa's premier collections of natural history and human cultural heritage. Its Whale Well — a six-storey atrium with full whale skeletons suspended from the ceiling — is one of Cape Town's most dramatic interior spaces. The San rock art collection, Stone Age artefacts and Gondwana palaeontology collections represent southern Africa's deep natural and human history across millions of years of time.
history: Founded in 1825 by Lord Charles Somerset, British Governor of the Cape Colony, making it Africa's oldest museum. During the apartheid era, the museum's practices regarding display of indigenous peoples' remains became a major source of controversy. The museum housed the remains of Saartjie Baartman, the Khoikhoi woman displayed in Europe as the 'Hottentot Venus,' until her remains were repatriated to South Africa in 2002 after years of diplomatic effort and advocacy by the South African government.

name: Tokyo National Museum
description: The Tokyo National Museum in Ueno Park is Japan's oldest and largest museum, housing over 120,000 works representing the full breadth of Japanese and Asian art and history. The museum's five buildings display samurai armour and swords, Buddhist sculpture, Jōmon pottery, ukiyo-e woodblock prints and treasures from the imperial household. The museum is also responsible for the conservation and display of over 88 National Treasures — Japan's most legally protected cultural objects, the crown jewels of Japanese civilisation.
history: Established in 1872 as part of Japan's first national Exposition in Ueno Park. The current Honkan main building, an Imperial Crown style structure designed by Jin Watanabe, was completed in 1938. The museum's collection was significantly expanded after World War II with artefacts collected across Asia during the imperial period. Repatriation discussions with South Korea and China over some of these objects remain ongoing and diplomatically sensitive between neighbouring nations.

name: Pergamon Museum
description: The Pergamon Museum on Berlin's Museum Island houses some of antiquity's greatest architectural monuments — reconstructed at full scale inside its vast halls. The Pergamon Altar (2nd century BC), the Market Gate of Miletus (2nd century AD) and the Ishtar Gate of Babylon (6th century BC) were excavated from their original sites in Turkey and Iraq and painstakingly reassembled block by block in Berlin, creating a museum experience of architectural immersion unlike anything else in the world.
history: The Pergamon Museum opened in 1930. The Ishtar Gate was excavated by Robert Koldewey between 1899 and 1914 and shipped to Berlin in 900 crates. During World War II the collection was stored in bunkers; some pieces were seized by Soviet forces and held in Russian museums until returned to East Germany in 1958. The museum is undergoing major renovation with its main wing closed until 2037 — the longest planned museum closure in modern history — raising profound questions about the future of these extraordinary reconstructed installations.

name: Musée d'Orsay
description: The Musée d'Orsay in Paris houses the world's greatest collection of Impressionist and Post-Impressionist masterpieces in a magnificent converted railway station on the Seine. Monet's water lilies, Renoir's Moulin de la Galette, Van Gogh's self-portraits, Degas' dancers and Toulouse-Lautrec's posters make the d'Orsay essential for lovers of 19th-century art. The station's original iron and glass architecture — a soaring cathedral of Victorian engineering — provides a dramatically beautiful setting that itself is part of the experience.
history: The Gare d'Orsay was built for the 1900 Paris Exposition Universelle but became obsolete for mainline rail by 1939 due to its short platforms, unsuitable for modern trains. The building was threatened with demolition before being converted to a museum that opened in 1986. The Impressionist collection came from the Louvre and the Jeu de Paume. Orson Welles filmed The Trial in the derelict building in 1962. The station's great clocks, visible from inside and outside, are among the most photographed objects in all of Paris.

name: Göbekli Tepe
description: Göbekli Tepe in southeastern Turkey is the world's oldest known monumental structure, built approximately 11,500 years ago — 7,000 years before Stonehenge. Its T-shaped limestone pillars weighing up to 20 tonnes are carved with animal reliefs and arranged in circular enclosures. Deliberately buried around 8,000 BC, its existence overturned the fundamental assumption that monumental architecture required settled agriculture — it was built entirely by hunter-gatherers who organised massive communal building projects before developing farming.
history: First examined in 1963 by archaeologists who dismissed it as a medieval cemetery. Klaus Schmidt began systematic excavation in 1994 and recognised its extraordinary prehistoric significance, working the site until his death in 2014. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 2018. Less than 5% of the site has been excavated — ground-penetrating radar surveys suggest up to 20 additional stone circles buried beneath the surrounding plateau, potentially making future discoveries even more transformative for our understanding of early human civilisation.

name: Machu Picchu
description: The 15th-century Inca citadel of Machu Picchu perches at 2,430 m above sea level in the Andes of Peru — one of the world's most dramatic archaeological sites. Built around 1450 at the height of the Inca Empire and abandoned a century later, its precise stonework, agricultural terraces and astronomical alignments reflect extraordinary engineering sophistication. UNESCO listed it in 1983 and it was declared a New Wonder of the World in 2007. Approximately 1 million visitors annually raise ongoing conservation concerns about the fragile site.
history: 'Discovered' by American historian Hiram Bingham III in 1911, guided to the site by local farmer Melchor Arteaga — though indigenous Quechua communities had always known of its existence. Bingham removed thousands of artefacts to Yale University, which returned them to Peru in 2012 after decades of diplomatic pressure. Current conservation concerns focus on the approximately 1 million annual visitors whose presence damages the fragile Inca stonework and surrounding cloud forest ecosystem, threatening the integrity of this irreplaceable heritage site.

name: Stonehenge
description: Stonehenge on Salisbury Plain is the world's most famous prehistoric monument — a ring of standing stones erected in multiple phases between 3000 and 1500 BC. The largest sarsen stones weigh up to 25 tonnes and were transported 25 km from Marlborough Downs. The bluestones originated in the Preseli Hills of Wales, 250 km distant. The monument is aligned with the midsummer sunrise and midwinter sunset, indicating sophisticated astronomical knowledge. Despite 200 years of investigation, its precise purpose remains actively debated by archaeologists and scientists worldwide.
history: Constructed in multiple phases over 1,500 years. Used as a burial ground for at least 500 years. UNESCO inscribed Stonehenge as part of the Stonehenge, Avebury and Associated Sites World Heritage Site in 1986. Modern Druid ceremonies were banned from 1985 to 2000 following conflicts with police; access is now permitted for solstice celebrations. A new visitor centre opened in 2013, and ongoing excavations continue to reveal new details about the monument's builders, purpose and the vast sacred landscape that surrounds it.

name: Great Pyramids of Giza
description: The Great Pyramids of Giza are the oldest of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World and the only ones still standing. The Great Pyramid of Khufu, completed around 2560 BC, stood as the world's tallest man-made structure for 3,800 years. The three pyramids of Khufu, Khafre and Menkaure, along with the Great Sphinx, represent the apex of ancient Egyptian engineering. The precision with which 2.3 million limestone blocks were quarried, transported and laid continues to astonish modern engineers and inspire competing scientific theories about construction methods.
history: Built during the Fourth Dynasty of ancient Egypt's Old Kingdom over approximately 85 years. Recent papyrus discoveries at Wadi al-Jarf document the logistics of pyramid construction including the transport of limestone by boat. UNESCO inscribed Memphis and its Necropolis as a World Heritage Site in 1979. The site faces ongoing threats from urban encroachment as Cairo's suburbs have expanded to the pyramid plateau boundary, creating one of the world's most dramatic juxtapositions of ancient monument and modern sprawling metropolis.

name: Angkor Wat
description: Angkor Wat is the world's largest religious monument at 162.6 hectares, and the best-preserved temple of the Khmer Empire. Built in the early 12th century by King Suryavarman II as a Hindu temple later converted to Buddhism, it features five lotus-shaped towers and 3 km of bas-relief galleries. At its peak, the Angkor complex housed up to one million people — the world's largest pre-industrial city — in a vast hydraulic civilisation that managed water across the Mekong floodplain using extraordinary engineering.
history: The Khmer Empire's capital from approximately the 9th to 15th centuries AD. Abandoned in 1431 following Thai invasions and climate-driven droughts. French naturalist Henri Mouhot 'rediscovered' the site for European audiences in 1860. UNESCO inscribed Angkor as a World Heritage Site in 1992 and coordinated restoration following the Khmer Rouge era devastation. LiDAR surveys since 2012 have revealed a vast hidden urban and hydraulic landscape surrounding the temples, showing the true scale of the Khmer civilisation.

name: Petra
description: Petra is the ancient Nabataean city carved into rose-red sandstone cliffs in southern Jordan. The Treasury (Al-Khazneh) with its 40-metre facade carved directly from the rock face is the iconic centrepiece of a city that controlled ancient spice and silk trade routes. Over 800 individual monuments including temples, tombs and a 6,000-seat theatre, all carved from living rock, were created by the Nabataean people between the 4th century BC and 2nd century AD in this extraordinary and awe-inspiring geological setting.
history: Served as the Nabataean Kingdom capital from around the 4th century BC, growing wealthy by taxing caravans. Declined after Roman annexation in 106 AD and an earthquake in 363 AD. Swiss explorer Johann Ludwig Burckhardt rediscovered it for Europeans in 1812, disguising himself as a Muslim pilgrim. UNESCO inscribed Petra in 1985 and it was named a New Wonder of the World in 2007. Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade (1989) featured its iconic facade, dramatically increasing global awareness of this extraordinary hidden city.

name: Chichen Itza
description: Chichen Itza was one of the greatest cities of Maya civilisation, flourishing between 600 and 1200 AD. The El Castillo pyramid casts a serpent shadow on its staircase at the spring and autumn equinoxes — a calendar in stone with 365 steps. The Sacred Cenote, Great Ball Court (the largest in ancient Mesoamerica), and the Observatory reflect the Maya's extraordinary knowledge of astronomy and mathematics. The site receives over 2 million visitors annually and remains Mexico's second most visited archaeological site after Teotihuacan.
history: Founded around 600 AD and reaching its height between 900–1200 AD. The Sacred Cenote has yielded hundreds of skeletal remains thrown in as ritual sacrifices. Largely abandoned by the time Spanish conquistadors arrived in the 16th century. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1988 and it was declared a New Wonder of the World in 2007. Restoration works over 60 years have stabilised the pyramids, though the methodology of reconstruction remains a subject of ongoing scientific debate among Mesoamerican archaeologists.

name: Colosseum
description: The Colosseum in Rome is the largest amphitheatre ever built — a 50,000-seat oval arena completed in 80 AD that hosted gladiatorial combat, animal hunts, public executions and theatrical performances for over 400 years. Its elliptical design, vaulted concrete construction and sophisticated crowd management system of 80 entrances made it an engineering masterpiece that has influenced stadium and arena design for two millennia. Two-thirds of the original structure remains standing despite centuries of earthquake damage and stone quarrying for other buildings.
history: Commissioned by Emperor Vespasian around 70 AD and completed by his son Titus in 80 AD, built on the site of Nero's private lake using 100,000 tonnes of travertine limestone. Gladiatorial combat continued until approximately 435 AD. The building was later used as a fortress, church, cemetery and quarry before recognition as a historic monument in the 19th century. Today it is Italy's most visited monument, receiving over 7 million visitors annually, and is one of the world's most recognised symbols of ancient Rome's power and engineering ambition.

name: Teotihuacan
description: Teotihuacan near Mexico City was one of the largest cities in the ancient world with perhaps 125,000 people at its 400 AD peak and the most influential urban centre in pre-Columbian Mesoamerica. Its Avenue of the Dead stretches 2.4 km flanked by the Pyramid of the Sun (third-largest pyramid in the world) and Pyramid of the Moon. The city's grid plan, apartment compounds, mural paintings and obsidian trade networks influenced civilisations across Mesoamerica for a thousand years after its mysterious collapse around 550 AD.
history: Founded around 100 BC, reaching its apex between 100–650 AD. Its builders and language are entirely unknown — the city predates the Aztec Empire by over a millennium. The Aztecs named it Teotihuacan ('birthplace of the gods') when they found it already ancient and abandoned, incorporating it into their cosmological mythology. UNESCO inscribed the site in 1987. Annual visitor numbers now exceed 4 million, making it Mexico's most visited archaeological site and one of the most significant pre-Columbian monuments in the entire Americas.

name: Olduvai Gorge
description: Olduvai Gorge (Oldupai) in Tanzania is one of the most important paleoanthropological sites in the world — a 48-km ravine that has yielded fossils documenting nearly 2 million years of human evolution. Louis and Mary Leakey discovered the skull of Paranthropus boisei in 1959 and remains of Homo habilis in 1960 — discoveries that shifted the scientific consensus on human origins definitively to Africa. The gorge exposes 2 million years of sedimentary layers, each containing fossils and stone tools charting progressive human technological development.
history: First documented scientifically by German entomologist Wilhelm Kattwinkel in 1911. Louis Leakey first visited in 1931 and began systematic work with his wife Mary in 1959. Their discovery of OH 5 (Paranthropus boisei) on 17 July 1959 made international headlines and secured National Geographic Society funding for decades of subsequent fieldwork. The Olduvai Gorge Museum at the site commemorates the Leakeys' work and displays original fossil casts at the exact location where humanity's origin story was permanently and dramatically rewritten.

name: Tikal
description: Tikal in Guatemala's Petén jungle was one of the most powerful cities of Classic Maya civilisation, with a population of perhaps 60,000 at its peak between 200 and 900 AD. Its six towering pyramids rise above the rainforest canopy — Temple IV at 65 m is the tallest pre-Columbian structure in the Americas. The site covers 576 km² with over 3,000 structures. Tikal waged centuries of warfare against rival Maya cities, and its history of alliance, conquest and political intrigue rivals any ancient civilisation in scale and complexity.
history: Occupied from as early as 800 BC and reaching its height in the Classic period. The city's collapse around 900 AD is attributed to warfare, drought, deforestation and political instability. First reported to the outside world in 1848 and systematic excavation began in the 1950s. UNESCO inscribed Tikal as a World Heritage Site in 1979. LiDAR technology in 2018 revealed a vast network of causeways, reservoirs and agricultural canals showing the true scale of the ancient Maya landscape was far greater than the visible above-ground monuments suggest.

name: Leptis Magna
description: Leptis Magna on Libya's Mediterranean coast is arguably the best-preserved Roman city in the world — its marble columns, triumphal arches, theatre, forum and baths standing in extraordinary completeness against the backdrop of the Mediterranean and the Sahara. Embellished to imperial magnificence by the Emperor Septimius Severus, who was born here in 145 AD, Leptis Magna represents the Roman world at its most architecturally ambitious. Sand dunes preserved it for centuries before Italian colonial-era excavations revealed this remarkable Mediterranean treasure.
history: Founded by Phoenician traders from Carthage around 1100 BC, flourishing as a trading port rich from olive oil and the wild animal trade. Emperor Septimius Severus beautified the city between 202–216 AD with a magnificent new forum, basilica and triumphal arch of extraordinary scale and sophistication. Declined after Arab conquest in 643 AD. UNESCO inscribed it as a World Heritage Site in 1982, though ongoing conflict in Libya has severely hampered site protection and conservation work in recent decades.

name: Persepolis
description: Persepolis was the ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Persian Empire — the ancient world's largest — built by Darius I beginning in 518 BC and destroyed by Alexander the Great in 330 BC. The Apadana Palace staircase reliefs depicting delegations from 23 subject nations bringing tribute represent one of antiquity's greatest sculptural programmes illustrating the sheer scale and diversity of the Persian world. The Gate of All Nations and Hundred-Column Hall demonstrate the organisational and artistic power of rulers whose empire stretched from Greece to India.
history: Construction began under Darius I around 518 BC on a rock terrace at Marvdasht. Burned by Alexander the Great in 330 BC — ancient sources debate whether it was deliberate revenge for Persian destruction of Athens or a drunken accident at a feast. UNESCO inscribed Persepolis as a World Heritage Site in 1979. Iran's Islamic Republic was initially hostile to preservation of pre-Islamic heritage but has since invested significantly in the site's conservation, recognising its importance to Iranian national identity and tourism revenue.

name: Skara Brae
description: Skara Brae on Orkney's Mainland island is northern Europe's most complete Neolithic village, occupied between 3100 and 2500 BC — older than both Stonehenge and the Great Pyramids. Eight stone houses connected by covered passageways are furnished with stone dressers, box beds, hearths and storage tanks surviving intact for 5,000 years. The village was buried under sand dunes after abandonment and rediscovered by a winter storm in 1850, giving an unparalleled and deeply intimate window into prehistoric domestic life.
history: Occupied for approximately 600 years before abandonment around 2500 BC. A catastrophic storm in 1850 stripped away the sand dunes, revealing the preserved houses. William Watt, owner of Skara Hall estate, began excavation that year. Further excavations by Gordon Childe in the 1920s established the site's Neolithic date and world significance. UNESCO inscribed Skara Brae as part of the Heart of Neolithic Orkney World Heritage Site in 1999. The site is one of Scotland's most visited prehistoric monuments and inspires awe at the sophistication of its 5,000-year-old builders.

name: Mohenjo-daro
description: Mohenjo-daro in Pakistan's Sindh province was one of the earliest and most sophisticated urban centres in the ancient world, built around 2500 BC at the height of the Indus Valley Civilisation. Its grid-planned streets, brick-built houses, covered drainage system, public granary and Great Bath represent urban planning not matched in Europe for another 4,000 years. The site once housed an estimated 40,000 people. The Indus script inscribed on thousands of seals remains completely undeciphered — one of archaeology's most tantalising and enduring mysteries.
history: Discovered in 1922 by Rakhal Das Banerji of the Archaeological Survey of India. R.E.M. Wheeler conducted major excavations in 1950. The city was abandoned around 1900 BC for reasons that remain actively debated — climate change, flooding and migration are all proposed with different degrees of supporting evidence. UNESCO inscribed the site in 1980 and placed it on the World Heritage in Danger list due to salt crystallisation damage threatening the mud-brick structures that have survived for four and a half millennia.

name: Çatalhöyük
description: Çatalhöyük in south-central Turkey is one of the world's oldest and best-preserved Neolithic towns, occupied between 7500 and 5700 BC by up to 8,000 people. Houses were entered through roof openings rather than street-level doors, and the dead were buried beneath the floors of lived-in homes. Wall paintings, figurines and animal skulls embedded in walls reflect a rich symbolic life. Remarkably, the town shows no evidence of social hierarchy — powerfully challenging assumptions about the inevitability of inequality in settled communities.
history: Excavated by James Mellaart between 1958 and 1965, whose discoveries of elaborate wall paintings and goddess figurines generated enormous popular interest in Neolithic religion. Ian Hodder led re-excavations from 1993, using post-processual archaeological methods that challenged some of Mellaart's interpretations. UNESCO inscribed Çatalhöyük as a World Heritage Site in 2012. A scandal over Mellaart's alleged forgery of Neolithic documents, revealed posthumously in 2018, has added significant controversy to the site's interpretive and academic history.

name: Karnak Temple Complex
description: The Karnak Temple Complex at Luxor is the largest religious building ever constructed — a 100-hectare complex built, expanded and modified by successive pharaohs over 1,500 years. The Hypostyle Hall contains 134 colossal columns arranged in 16 rows, each reaching 23 metres high and 10 metres in circumference. The walls covered in hieroglyphic inscriptions document the political and religious history of ancient Egypt in extraordinary detail, recording three millennia of continuous building by at least 30 successive pharaohs across Egypt's greatest dynasties.
history: Karnak was the most important temple complex of ancient Egypt and the power base of the Amun priesthood, who at their peak controlled more wealth than the pharaoh himself. Construction began under Senusret I around 2000 BC and continued until Roman times. An active place of worship for over 1,500 years. UNESCO inscribed ancient Thebes, including Karnak, as a World Heritage Site in 1979. The site attracts over 2 million visitors annually and is Egypt's second most visited ancient monument after the Pyramids of Giza.

name: Jenne-jeno
description: Jenne-jeno (Ancient Djenné) in Mali is the earliest known city in sub-Saharan Africa, occupied from around 250 BC to 1400 AD, predating European contact by over a millennium. The archaeological mound reveals 2,000 years of continuous occupation including iron smelting, terracotta figurines and trade goods from North Africa and the Sahara — demonstrating that complex urban civilisations developed independently in West Africa without external influence. The living city of Djenné beside it has the world's largest mud-brick building, the magnificent Great Mosque.
history: Excavations by Roderick and Susan McIntosh in the 1970s and 1980s overturned the longstanding assumption that sub-Saharan Africa had no pre-colonial urban civilisation. Jenne-jeno showed that African cities developed organically from local trade networks. UNESCO inscribed the Old Towns of Djenné as a World Heritage Site in 1988. The Great Mosque was placed on the UNESCO World Heritage in Danger list in 2016 due to political instability and looting affecting the wider Sahel region in which this extraordinary site is located.

name: Caral
description: Caral in Peru's Supe Valley is the oldest known city in the Americas, dated to approximately 2600 BC — contemporary with Egypt's Old Kingdom and Mesopotamia's Ur III period. The site contains six large platform mounds (pyramids), sunken circular plazas, residential quarters and evidence of sophisticated social organisation including textiles and quipu (knotted-string record-keeping). Caral demonstrates that complex civilisation emerged independently in the Americas at the same time as in the Old World, with no contact between them — a profound statement about human ingenuity.
history: Excavated by Peruvian archaeologist Ruth Shady beginning in 1994. Carbon-14 dating confirmed its extraordinary age in 2001, triggering major revision of the timeline of American civilisation. UNESCO inscribed Caral as a World Heritage Site in 2009. Ruth Shady's work transformed scientific understanding of the Americas' independent development of complex urban civilisation and established her as one of South America's most important living archaeologists. The site faces ongoing threats from illegal squatters and agricultural expansion on its periphery.

name: Aksum
description: Aksum in northern Ethiopia was the capital of the Aksumite Empire, one of the ancient world's great powers, dominating trade between the Mediterranean and Indian Ocean from the 1st to 7th centuries AD. Its monumental stelae — granite obelisks up to 33 metres tall, some standing after 2,000 years — are among the most impressive monuments of ancient Africa. Aksum is also believed by Ethiopian tradition to house the Ark of the Covenant in the Church of Our Lady Mary of Zion, making it one of Christianity's most sacred and mysterious pilgrimage destinations.
history: The Aksumite Empire controlled the strategic Red Sea trade route, minting its own gold coins — one of only three empires worldwide to do so in antiquity alongside Rome and Persia. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980. One of Aksum's greatest obelisks was looted by Italian colonial forces in 1937 and erected in Rome. After decades of diplomatic effort, it was returned to Ethiopia and re-erected in Aksum in 2008 — one of Africa's most significant and hard-won cultural repatriation successes.
